name: Falklands War 1982
description: A map-first notebook of the 1982 Falklands campaign, showing how sea control, air reach, and narrow landing geography shaped the British recovery of the islands.

sides.name: United Kingdom
sides.description: British forces had to combine political signaling, sea control, amphibious lift, and a final ground advance over austere terrain.

groups.name: Task force and landing force
groups.description: The British campaign depended on a carrier-centered fleet, the staging network behind it, and the brigade that would eventually come ashore.

subUnits.name: Task Force 317
subUnits.description:
Carrier-led expeditionary force built to recover the islands and establish sea and air access long enough for an amphibious landing.
- Role: Long-range maritime strike and command force
- Geographic anchor: Moved from the North Atlantic staging chain through Ascension Island into waters east of the Falklands.
- Why it matters: It linked politics to action; without it there was no credible way to isolate the islands or protect the landing force.
- What to watch: Carrier aircraft availability, submarine effects, and vulnerability while massed around the landing area.

media.caption: HMS Hermes and HMS Broadsword during the South Atlantic campaign.

subUnits.name: 3 Commando Brigade
subUnits.description:
The brigade that translated British sea access into a land campaign, first through the San Carlos landing and then by pushing east toward Stanley.
- Role: Amphibious and light infantry ground force
- Geographic anchor: Concentrated around San Carlos Water and later moved along the East Falkland ridgelines toward the capital.
- Why it matters: Its movement from beachhead to high ground turned an amphibious lodgment into a campaign with a clear end state.
- What to watch: How the beachhead, helicopter lift, and terrain combined to set the tempo of the final approach.

sides.name: Argentina
sides.description: Argentina held the islands but had to defend a distant garrison under growing maritime isolation while sustaining air attack from the mainland.

groups.name: Island garrison and mainland air arm

subUnits.name: Argentine Falklands Garrison
subUnits.description:
The island defense centered on Port Stanley, local positions, and the need to absorb British pressure from the beachhead inward.
- Role: Fixed defense and local maneuver force
- Geographic anchor: Anchored on Stanley and the ridgelines west of the town, with limited freedom to reposition under British artillery and air pressure.
- Why it matters: Once the British landed, the garrison became a race between defensive cohesion and encirclement.
- What to watch: Ground held, mobility available, and whether mainland support could still change the balance.

subUnits.name: Rio Grande Strike Wing
subUnits.description:
Mainland air power gave Argentina its sharpest operational reach, but the distance to the islands narrowed loiter time and made every sortie count.
- Role: Strike aircraft staging and launch network
- Geographic anchor: Based around Rio Grande in Tierra del Fuego and projected west-to-east toward shipping off East Falkland.
- Why it matters: Its attacks forced the British fleet to disperse, improvise air defense, and accept risk around the landing area.
- What to watch: Sortie density, weapon effectiveness, and how distance compressed tactical options.

events.title: Argentina seizes the Falklands
events.description: Argentine forces landed and took control of the islands, forcing London to decide whether it could assemble and sustain a recovery campaign across the South Atlantic.

events.title: Britain declares a maritime exclusion zone
events.description: The exclusion zone was a military and political signal. It narrowed the legal and operational space around the islands and set the stage for submarine-led pressure on Argentine naval movement.

events.title: ARA General Belgrano is sunk
events.description: The sinking helped push Argentina's surface fleet back toward port and made the maritime contest increasingly about air attack and British fleet protection.

events.title: British land at San Carlos Water
events.description: The landing transformed the campaign. From this point onward the British challenge was to hold the beachhead under air attack and build a ground line of advance toward Stanley.

events.title: Goose Green confirms British momentum
events.description: The fight at Goose Green mattered beyond its scale. It signaled that the British could move inland, fight set-piece battles, and hold the initiative under difficult logistics.

events.title: Argentine forces surrender at Stanley
events.description: After the British advance onto the heights west of town, the campaign ended with the surrender of Argentine forces in Stanley and the reestablishment of British control.

meta.name: Ascension Island staging node
meta.description: The island was the critical intermediate base that connected Britain to the South Atlantic theater.

meta.name: Total Exclusion Zone
meta.description: The 200-nautical-mile exclusion zone illustrated how a legal declaration could reshape operational behavior on the water.

meta.name: San Carlos Water
meta.description: A constricted landing area that gave the British a workable amphibious entry point while still exposing ships to intense air attack.

meta.name: Port Stanley
meta.description: Stanley was the administrative and symbolic objective whose fall would close the campaign.

meta.name: Rio Grande strike base
meta.description: Mainland launch point for Argentine air attacks that imposed continuing risk on British ships near the islands.
